video_title: Poolside’s Model Factory, Laguna S, Open Models, and the Race to AGI — Eiso Kant, Poolside AI
gist: Poolside co-founder Eiso Kant details the company's shift to open-sourcing weights AND research, its 'model factory' that compresses model builds to 5-8 weeks, and the release of Laguna S (118B total / 8B active), arguing that persistence-type behaviors — not raw parameter count — are unlocking small-model capability and pointing toward commoditized, open intelligence rather than a 2-3 player oligopoly.

insights.claim: Poolside is open-sourcing model weights AND full technical/research reports (not just weights) to actively prevent an 'oligopoly of intelligence' controlled by a handful of labs, positioning itself as a Western open-research lab distinct from Chinese labs that release weights but less research.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Signals a deliberate commoditization play; open weights + reproducible research from a funded Western lab pressures closed-model pricing and lowers the barrier for new foundation-model entrants.
insights.quote: I rather live in a world that has a 100 foundation model companies than a world that has five. even if I was one of the five.

insights.claim: Kant argues AI investors are now split primarily by whether they believe intelligence will be an 'oligopoly' controllable by two players or one breakable by many companies, and says the two-player camp was much larger at the start of 2026 than it is now.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Frames the core disagreement underpinning foundation-model valuations; a shift away from the winner-take-two view supports capital flowing to neolabs and open-model plays.
insights.quote: who believes that it's going to be an igopoly of intelligence and who believes that igopoly can be broken by other companies and I think that's what divides investors more than anything else

insights.claim: Kant contends intelligence will become the world's most demanded commodity and will commoditize in margin and price, making open source a viable path to win rather than a charity project.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: A commoditization thesis argues against durable pricing power for closed frontier labs and for margin compression across the model layer over time.

insights.claim: Kant is actively encouraging researchers to leave and start competing foundation-model companies, citing a closing window before recursive self-improvement makes catching up to incumbents unfeasible.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Implies a hard time-limited window for new entrants; capital deployed into foundation-model startups may need to move before an RSI-driven divergence in capability.
insights.quote: we've got a small window before models are really impacting recursive self-improvement to a level where catching up otherwise might become unfeasible

insights.claim: Poolside is characterized as one of roughly 20 (not 100) Western 'neolabs,' with the host noting most open-source innovation historically came from Chinese labs and the only Western 'DeepSeek of the West' candidates being thinking machines and reflection.
insights.speaker: Vibhu (co-host)
insights.so_what: Maps the competitive field of open Western foundation-model labs — a short list worth tracking for capability breakthroughs and acquisition interest.

themes.theme: Model Factory as the Real Moat

insights.claim: Poolside frames model building as ~90% engineering and has built an industrialized 'model factory' (from a handful to thousands of components) treating the factory — not any single model — as the durable asset, analogous to SpaceX's factory or Foxconn.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Reframes the moat from any given checkpoint to reproducible pipeline infrastructure — the asset investors should diligence in a model company.

insights.claim: The factory's optimized metric is wall-clock speed of an idea from a researcher to a trusted experimental result that then enters the next training run, with changes from the prior day making it into runs immediately (no 90-day data cutoff).
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Iteration latency, not GPU count, is the stated competitive variable — a differentiator that compounds and is hard for slower-cadence labs to match.
insights.quote: the model should be an artifact of someone's process it shouldn't be really a thing in itself

insights.claim: Poolside streams data just-in-time into training over an immutable data layer with experiments-as-code, giving perfect reproducibility (able to reproduce runs from two years ago) and treating data mixing as config via an internal service called 'Blender.'
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Reproducibility and streaming data remove repackaging bottlenecks and enable rigorous ablations — an infrastructure edge that accelerates the research loop.

insights.claim: Poolside's pre-training data selection is automated via an 'automixer' that trains eight small models, scales them up, and picks the pre-training dataset with minimal human review.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Automated data-mix selection is a concrete example of the engineering rigor that lets a sub-70-researcher team compete on data quality.

insights.claim: Reliability is treated as core infra: Laguna S had zero on-call events and Poolside reports no meaningful wake-up on-call event all year, aside from occasional first-6-hour config errors on a new run.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Operational reliability at this level is a proxy for pipeline maturity and lets a tiny team run 10-20k experiments a month without firefighting.

insights.claim: Agents increasingly run Poolside's model factory — writing code, launching jobs, evaluating model-run results — especially on data pipelines and increasingly architecture, which Kant frames as early 'twinklings' of recursive self-improvement.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: A concrete instance of AI accelerating its own R&D; if generalizable, it favors labs with agent-ready internal tooling and compounds capability gaps.

insights.claim: Poolside's build cadence has collapsed to weeks: Laguna XS2 took 5 weeks from start of pre-training to launch and Laguna S took 8 weeks, with the next model starting the day post-training finished; model releases are now counted in weeks, not months or years.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: A weeks-not-months release cadence means faster capability iteration and quicker response to competitors — a schedule metric investors can benchmark across labs.

themes.theme: Small Models & Behaviors Over Raw Intelligence

insights.claim: Laguna S is a 118B-total / 8B-active sparse model (3:1 sliding-window-to-global attention, trained in FP8) that fits on an NVIDIA DGX Spark at 30-40 tokens/sec, solves Erdos problem 397 independently, and outperforms models two-to-three times its size on coding tasks.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: A small, single-device-capable model beating far larger ones on coding validates the small-model efficiency thesis and pressures the economics of large closed models.

insights.claim: Poolside attributes Laguna S's gains to model behavior — more verification, persistence, backtracking, and not declaring victory early — rather than raw intelligence, per co-head of applied research Peng, and says these gains are entirely from post-training.
insights.speaker: Peng, Poolside co-head of applied research (quoted by Eiso Kant)
insights.so_what: If behavior (not scale) drives most usable capability, post-training recipes become the key differentiator and small models capture more of the knowledge-work market.
insights.quote: I have the feeling that a lot of the gains in Laguna s come not from more intelligence, but more from different behavior, more verification, less taking things for granted, not declaring victory early, and being way more persistent.

insights.claim: Kant argues most knowledge work (~25% of global economy, ~$25 trillion) rewards persistence rather than cross-domain idea synthesis, so the optimal model size for knowledge work peaks and bigger-model ROI turns negative beyond it — he's already 'sizing down' models for certain tasks.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: A ceiling on useful model size for most economic work implies demand shifts toward cheaper mid-size models, undercutting the case for endlessly scaling frontier inference.

insights.claim: Kant now suspects the model-size 'peak' for solving knowledge work may be as low as 1-5-10 trillion parameters rather than requiring two-to-three more orders of magnitude, which he frames as a direct argument for model commoditization and open source winning.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: If frontier knowledge-work capability saturates at a few trillion params, the marginal value of the largest models drops and open mid-size models capture the bulk of enterprise demand.

insights.claim: Kant distinguishes that true frontier science (e.g. Einstein connecting distant ideas) does benefit from larger, more intelligent models, even as most knowledge work does not — so scaling still matters for science/medicine/bio breakthroughs.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Bifurcates the market: bet on small models for commoditized knowledge work, but frontier scientific/discovery applications remain a premium use case for the largest models.

insights.claim: Kant explicitly warns against Poolside becoming 'king of open-source small models,' calling it a copout, and insists they must scale models as large as competitors to succeed.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Tempers the small-model narrative — Poolside intends to compete at frontier scale, so the small-model story is a signal of efficiency, not a strategic ceiling.

insights.claim: Kant holds a not-commonly-held view that reinforcement learning will move earlier and earlier into pre-training (before even mid-training), and that 'mid-training' is just a crude two-stage curriculum and an organizational artifact that will dissolve into a continuous curriculum as compute grows.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Signals where training methodology is heading; labs that collapse discrete training stages into a continuous curriculum could gain compute efficiency and capability edges.

insights.claim: Kant argues next-token-prediction pre-training alone is not enough and that there is a 'huge amount of gold' left in the web if it can be turned into signal that teaches models to think earlier in training — work Poolside has spent years on but keeps closed.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Points to the next pre-training objective beyond next-token prediction as a live frontier; a breakthrough here would be a durable capability advantage.

insights.claim: Kant is skeptical that scaling up ever more RL 'environments' is the path to AGI, calling environments and distillation 'drugs' the industry is addicted to, and questioning a never-ending environment-building approach.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Contrarian caution against the environments-heavy RL consensus; if right, capital pouring into RL-environment tooling may be over-indexed.

insights.claim: Poolside deliberately does NOT distill from a larger unshipped model; its released models are 'dual-purpose' (research signal plus shipped product), and frequent from-scratch training reveals which changes drove improvements — versus a 6-month cadence that produces an uninterpretable 'soup.'
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: A distinct strategy from frontier labs that ship distilled models; frequent from-scratch runs trade some efficiency for cleaner scientific attribution and faster learning.

themes.theme: Harnesses, Tools & the Death of MCP

insights.claim: Kant argues MCP and tool-calling are the wrong abstraction — models should write code (with loops and conditionals) against a VM/container with binaries and codebases rather than call 50 tools stuffed in a system prompt, and predicts within 12 months no system prompt will be stuffed with 20-40 tools.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: A directional call against the MCP/tool-registry paradigm; if correct, it reshapes agent tooling toward code-execution sandboxes and away from tool-definition ecosystems.
insights.quote: I think we will in 12 months not see a single system prompt that is stuffed with 20 or 30 or 40 tools anymore.

insights.claim: Poolside's Pool CLI harness is deliberately minimal (~6 tools: shell, shell kill, shell wait, write, fetch web, bash) and Laguna S is Poolside's first model to support parallel tool calling.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: A minimal-harness philosophy (compared to Pi) contrasts with heavy agent harnesses; signals Poolside is betting model capability, not harness engineering, carries the load.

insights.claim: Kant frames that a model company's own harness will always beat others because RL compute and synthetic data are spent there, and Poolside does light 'multi-harness polishing' so capabilities transfer to third-party harnesses like open code and Kilo Code.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Explains why benchmark parity across harnesses is hard; buyers evaluating a model should test it in their own harness, not just the vendor's.

insights.claim: Poolside identifies strictly as a model company (not an agent company), while conceding models can no longer be trained without a harness attached — the harness is now bundled with the weights the way a container once was.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Clarifies the layer Poolside is competing in; agent companies can do more on top, leaving room for a distinct application/agent layer above the model.

insights.claim: Poolside chose coding and long-horizon software tasks as its path to AGI because they force solving hard reasoning, tool-use, and long-horizon problems, and Kant reports the skills generalize — Laguna S does well at deep research and even legal-bench despite no targeted training.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: A coding-first route to general capability; positive transfer to unrelated domains supports the bet that coding models can expand into legal/medical verticals cheaply.

insights.claim: Kant identifies RL as batch-size-constrained (finite tasks, unlike the whole web for pre-training), so RL compute cannot be scaled like pre-training, making RL wall-clock time Poolside's biggest current bottleneck.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Identifies a structural limit on scaling RL; solving it (via disaggregation) is where Kant sees the biggest speed-up lever, a signal for hardware/infra investors.

insights.claim: Kant is excited about bringing prefill/decode disaggregation and mixed hardware (general GPUs plus specialized inference chips like Groq's LPU) into RL training to break the batch-size constraint and cut wall-clock time.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Suggests demand for heterogeneous training hardware and PD-disaggregation infra; a potential tailwind for specialized inference-chip vendors moving into training.

insights.claim: Kant states the compute bottleneck is memory and networking, not raw FLOPs, asserting that with infinite compute and networking you'd 'probably be at AGI tomorrow.'
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Points investment attention to memory bandwidth and interconnect/networking as the binding constraints rather than pure GPU count.
insights.quote: if you had infinite comput you'd be at AGI probably already tomorrow

insights.claim: Kant claims the hardware generation gap between Hopper and GB300 is the difference between being able to train a ~1 trillion-parameter model and a 5-6 trillion-parameter model, explaining the step-function progress tied to each NVIDIA generation.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Quantifies how model scale is gated by hardware generation — a concrete lens for timing capability jumps to NVIDIA's product cadence.

insights.claim: Poolside is a '10k H200 cluster' company currently training on Hoppers in FP8, planning to scale to more compute and move to NVFP4 training on Blackwell, and Kant calls NVIDIA's Nemotron NVFP4 training work underrated.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Discloses Poolside's compute scale and precision roadmap; low-precision (NVFP4/ternary) training could reset compute economics and is a watch-item for efficiency gains.

insights.claim: Kant asserts NVIDIA effectively holds more power than the US government over who can build foundation models because chip allocations determine access to compute, with TSMC below that.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Frames chip allocation as the true chokepoint on the AI value chain, reinforcing NVIDIA/TSMC as the structural gatekeepers investors should weight.

insights.claim: Kant argues deciding in 2026, based on current model capabilities, that only two or three companies can build frontier models would be a dystopian concentration of power, and that democratic government — not unilateral company decisions — should set limits on model misuse.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: A policy stance that open-model incumbency shouldn't be locked in by early regulation; relevant to regulatory-risk pricing for open vs closed labs.

insights.claim: Kant calls it 'insane' and 'wild' that the industry accepts model providers banning use of their models for foundation-model development, while conceding it is legal capitalism.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Highlights competitive restrictions in model ToS as a moat-building tactic by incumbents — a friction point for neolabs bootstrapping off closed models.

insights.claim: Kant draws an analogy that banning cigarette advertising, though well-intentioned, entrenched a tobacco oligopoly by blocking new competitors, warning heavy-handed blanket AI restrictions would similarly cement today's incumbents.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: A vivid regulatory-capture warning; blanket open-model restrictions would be a bullish structural moat for incumbents and a bearish signal for new entrants.
insights.quote: it effectively established an igopoly of cigarette companies because no one else could ever compete

insights.claim: Kant advocates regulating specific, internationally-agreed dangerous capabilities rather than binary 'open-or-closed' model rules, and argues unilateral safety fails because competitors won't pause — so global coordination is required.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Capability-based, coordinated regulation is the framework Kant expects; safety-institute and eval infrastructure are the beneficiaries to watch.

insights.claim: Poolside raised a $500 million round about 1.5 years ago when most investor conversations were still about whether models were 'stochastic parrots,' and Kant argues the count of AGI-believers is now growing super-linearly.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Marks how fast the investor Overton window on AGI has shifted; belief expansion supports continued mega-rounds for foundation-model startups.

insights.claim: Poolside built Laguna S with fewer than 70 researchers and about 35 engineers (under 150 people total), which Kant pitches as the highest impact-per-individual ratio available versus large labs where the denominator is huge.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Demonstrates a competitive frontier-ish model built by a tiny team — a capital-efficiency data point and a recruiting lever against larger labs.

insights.claim: Kant names agency as the single most important hiring signal in the AI age and argues high-agency people are best aligned via a common goal plus explicit boundaries ('lanes') such as no-multimodal and RL focus, rather than open-ended freedom.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: A management framework for AI-native orgs; constraint-based alignment of high-agency talent is presented as the antidote to big-tech 'exploration algorithm' politics.

insights.claim: Kant argues constraints — relatively little compute, little money, and deliberately little purchased external data — forced Poolside to develop efficiency advantages on other axes that better-resourced competitors lacked.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Suggests capital scarcity can be an innovation forcing-function; relevant when comparing lean neolabs against heavily-funded incumbents.

insights.claim: Kant proposes the core engineering-productivity metric in the AI era is lead time from an identified valuable idea to shipped value plus how few people are involved, with AI-native orgs designed around a single builder/IC close to the customer rather than large teams.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: A framework for how AI reshapes org design toward smaller headcount per unit of shipped value — a lens for evaluating operating leverage in software companies.

insights.claim: Poolside deliberately avoided hiring researchers in the Bay Area early, betting on a global talent war and building a fully-remote company with talent from middle America, Seattle, Serbia, Taiwan and Singapore plus offices in Paris and London.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: A distributed-talent strategy as competitive differentiation; validates that frontier-capable research talent exists well outside Silicon Valley.

insights.claim: Kant reframes the 'DeepSeek moment' as a falsehood about cost — arguing the training run itself is cheap and 'anticlimactic,' while the expensive part is the years of R&D, infrastructure and data behind it.
insights.speaker: Eiso Kant (guest, Poolside CEO)
insights.so_what: Corrects the market's over-indexing on cheap headline training-run costs; the real capex/moat is the accumulated factory and R&D, not the final run.
insights.quote: the training run is not the expensive part. The training run is a very anticlimactic event
